responses_by_run.answer: Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena, disguised as an old woman, advised Arachne to show humility and ask the goddess for pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected her and challenged the goddess to a weaving contest [1]. After Arachne lost the contest, Athena was enraged that the mortal's work was flawless and showed disrespect to the gods [1]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly [1]. When Arachne attempted to hang herself in despair, Athena saved her life but pronounced a curse: Arachne would dangle on a cord forever, and her punishment would extend to all her descendants [2]. Athena then sprinkled Arachne with a magical herb extract, transforming her body into that of a spider, condemning her to weave webs for eternity [3].

Prometheus was punished for two primary offenses. First, he stole fire and gave it to humans [1]. According to Apollodorus, Prometheus hid fire in a stalk of fennel and gave it to men, and when Zeus learned of this theft, he ordered Hephaestus to nail Prometheus to Mount Caucasus, where an eagle devoured his liver daily [1]. Second, Hesiod indicates that Prometheus was also punished for attempting to deceive Zeus by unfairly dividing portions of an ox at Mecone, presenting Zeus with bones covered in fat while keeping the better meat for humans [2]. His punishment involved being bound with inextricable bonds and having a long-winged eagle eat his immortal liver, which would regenerate each night [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and left him alone [1][2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and had his men cling to the undersides of the larger rams [3][4]. Odysseus himself grasped the largest ram and clung to its belly [3][4]. When Polyphemus opened the cave door in the morning to let his flocks out to pasture, he felt the sheep as they passed but could not detect the men hidden beneath them [3][4]. Odysseus and his comrades thus escaped with the sheep to their ship [3][4].

responses_by_run.answer: At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis and held her fast despite her shape-shifting into fire, water, and various beasts, until she resumed her original form [1]. As wedding gifts, Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear [1], and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1].

citations.author: Apollodorus
citations.stance: describes the wedding celebration, Thetis's capture, and the gifts given to Peleus

responses_by_run.answer: Demeter stopped the crops from growing because she was grieving and yearning for her daughter Persephone, who had been taken away [1]. She vowed that she would never let fruit spring out of the ground until she beheld her daughter again [2].

The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of the Titan Cronus.

SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Cronus' AND child.type = 'olympian'

responses_by_run.answer: Several heroes are identified as children of Zeus in classical sources. Aeneas [1] is described as a son of Zeus, as is Arcesilaus [2]. Additionally, Iasion [3] is counted among Zeus's children.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,

FROM relationships r

JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id

responses_by_run.answer:
Perseus encountered several monsters during his adventures. He slew the Gorgon Medusa and beheaded her [1]. He also encountered the Phorcides, the three old women sisters of the Gorgons [2]. During his journey, Perseus witnessed men and animals turned to stone by Medusa's visage [3].

Beyond his direct confrontation with Medusa, Perseus used the Gorgon's head as a weapon against other adversaries. He turned Phineus and his fellow conspirators to stone by showing them the Gorgon's head [4]. Similarly, he turned Polydectes and his gathered friends to stone by displaying the Gorgon's head with averted face [5].

In the battle at Seriphus, Perseus encountered and slew various human opponents including Erithus (son of Actor), Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, and Phlegias [2]. He also slew Molpeus and Ethemon during Phineus's attack [3].

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a primordial deity [1], and from Earth came Cronus [2], who became Zeus's father. Cronus is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod presents him in his cosmological framework [2], while Apollodorus documents him in his mythographic handbook [3]. Homer also references Cronus in his poetic accounts [4][5], and the Homeric Hymns preserve traditions about this Titan [6]. Thus the lineage proceeds from the primordial Chaos through Earth and Cronus to Zeus, establishing the genealogical foundation of Olympian rule.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Zeus'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM lineage
LEFT JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

WITH heroes_with_divine_parents AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT child.id, child.name
    FROM relationships r
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
    WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of'
      AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
      AND child.type = 'hero'
)
SELECT DISTINCT h.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, vc.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM heroes_with_divine_parents h
JOIN variant_claims vc ON vc.subject_entity_id = h.id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = vc.source_id
WHERE vc.claim_type = 'death'
  AND vc.claim_value ILIKE '%Troy%'

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage to Zeus is traced through his father's line as follows: Zeus fathered Aeacus with the mortal Aegina [1]. Aeacus then had a son Peleus with the mortal Endeis, daughter of Sciron [1]. Peleus married Thetis, a goddess and member of the Nereids [1], and together they became the parents of Achilles [1]. Thus Achilles was the grandson of Aeacus and great-grandson of Zeus [1], with his mother being divine while his father's line traced back to Zeus through Aeacus [1].
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.type, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Achilles'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, parent.type, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.depth AS generation_level,
       lineage.name AS entity_name,
       lineage.type AS entity_type,
       s.author AS author,
       s.work AS work,
       s.stance AS stance,
       lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref
FROM lineage
JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
WHERE lineage.source_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage varies across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite was the child of Dione [1], while other Homeric works present a different account: the Odyssey identifies her as the daughter of Zeus [2]. Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca similarly records conflicting traditions, describing her as both a child of Zeus and a child of Dione [3]. Hesiod's Theogony offers a distinct origin story, stating that Aphrodite was born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [4]. The Homeric Hymns also identify her as the daughter of Zeus [5], as do other Homeric passages [6] and later sources such as Ovid's Metamorphoses [7].

responses_by_run.answer: Io's father is identified differently across ancient sources. According to Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca, Io was the daughter of Iasus [1], though Apollodorus also notes that Hesiod and Acusilaus claimed she was the daughter of Piren [2], while Castor and many tragedians identified her father as Inachus [3]. Ovid, in the Metamorphoses, consistently presents Io as the daughter of Inachus [4].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by an arrow shot by Paris, the Trojan prince, guided by the god Apollo [1]. According to Ovid, Apollo revealed himself to Paris and pointed out Achilles, guiding Paris's arrow to strike the hero fatally [2]. Apollodorus notes that Achilles had been forewarned by his mother Thetis that he would die by Apollo's hand if he killed Tenes, which he did anyway [3]. Homer's account similarly foretells that Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [4], and that Thetis had warned him he would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [5]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle [1].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his prize, Briseis. According to Homer's account, Achilles was angry that Agamemnon threatened to take away his prize—the reward he had earned through his efforts in battle—while Agamemnon's own prize remained far greater [1]. Achilles declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain dishonored and toil to pile up riches for Agamemnon [1]. Apollodorus's account more simply states that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of the priest Chryses [2].

Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was an extraordinarily skilled weaver who refused to acknowledge that her talent came from Athena or to show the goddess proper respect [1]. When Athena challenged her to a weaving contest, Arachne not only competed but wove a tapestry depicting the gods' shameful deeds, which enraged the goddess [1]. After Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly, Arachne attempted suicide [2]. Athena saved her from death but pronounced a cruel eternal punishment: Arachne would be transformed into a spider, forced to dangle on a cord forever and weave endlessly as a reminder of her presumption [2].

Prometheus was punished for two main transgressions. First, he stole fire and gave it to humans [1]. According to Apollodorus, when Zeus learned that Prometheus had hidden fire in a fennel stalk and given it to humans, Zeus ordered Hephaestus to bind him to Mount Caucasus, where an eagle devoured his liver daily [1]. Second, according to Hesiod, Prometheus attempted to deceive Zeus during a dispute over sacrificial portions at Mecone by arranging the portions unfairly, setting flesh and fat for mortals while giving Zeus only bones dressed in fat [2][3]. For these acts of deception and defiance against Zeus's will, Prometheus was bound with inextricable chains and subjected to the eternal torment of the eagle consuming his liver [2][3].

Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus called out to other Cyclopes for help, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and departed [2]. Second, after the blinding, Odysseus tied the Cyclops's sheep together in groups of three and hid himself beneath the largest ram by clinging to its fleece [3]. When Polyphemus opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, he felt the sheep as they passed but could not detect the men hidden beneath them [3]. Odysseus and his surviving comrades thus escaped with the sheep to their ship [3].

At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear as a wedding gift, and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis despite her shape-shifting resistance, holding her fast as she transformed into fire, water, and beasts until she resumed her original form [1].

The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of the Titan Cronus.

responses_by_run.answer: Based on the provided texts, Perseus encountered the following monsters: (1) the Gorgon (Medusa), whom he slew and beheaded [1]; (2) Pegasus and his brother, who were born from Medusa's blood after her death [1][2]. responses_by_run.answer: Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to primordial chaos. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a fundamental divine force [1]. From Earth came Cronus [2], who became the ruler of the Titans. Cronus is attested across multiple sources: Hesiod describes him in his cosmological framework [3], while Homer presents him in poetic contexts within the Iliad [4] and Odyssey [5]. The Homeric Hymns also reference Cronus [6]. Apollodorus, in his mythographic handbook, systematically records that Cronus descended from Earth [7], establishing the genealogical chain that ultimately leads to Zeus as Cronus's son and heir to divine rule.

WITH troy_deaths AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT vc.subject_entity_id
    FROM variant_claims vc
    WHERE vc.claim_type = 'death' AND vc.claim_value ILIKE '%Troy%'
)
SELECT DISTINCT h.name AS hero_name,
       parent.name AS divine_parent,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM entities h
JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = h.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
JOIN troy_deaths td ON td.subject_entity_id = h.id
WHERE h.type = 'hero'
  AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
ORDER BY h.name ASC, parent.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage to Zeus traces through both his paternal and maternal lines. On his father's side, Peleus was the grandson of Zeus, descended through Aeacus, who was the son of Zeus and the nymph Aegina [1]. Peleus married Thetis, a Nereid and daughter of Nereus [1], making her a goddess in her own right. This union produced Achilles, who thus became the grandson of Zeus through his father's line [1] while also being the son of a goddess through his mother [1]. One source emphasizes this dual divine heritage, noting that Peleus was 'grandson of great Jove' (Zeus) and was 'honored with a goddess for a wife,' underscoring how Achilles inherited divine status from both sides of his family [2][3].

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20

       e.type AS entity_type,
       s.author AS author,
       s.work AS work,

FROM lineage

LEFT JOIN entities e ON e.id = lineage.id
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is described differently across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite was the child of Dione [1], while other Homeric sources present her as the daughter of Zeus [2]. Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca identifies her as a child of both Zeus and Dione [3][4]. However, Hesiod in the Theogony offers a distinctly different account, describing Aphrodite as born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [5]. The Homeric Hymns similarly identify her as the daughter of Zeus [6], as do multiple passages in Homer's Odyssey [7][8]. Later, Ovid in the Metamorphoses also presents her as a child of Zeus [9].

SELECT parent.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id

responses_by_run.answer:
Achilles died by being struck with an arrow. According to Homer, he was fated to be slain in battle, and the dying Hector foretold that he would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [1]. Homer also indicates that Thetis foretold he would perish beneath the wall of the Trojans by the swift missiles of Apollo [2].

Ovid describes how Apollo, at Neptune's instigation, guided Paris's arrow to strike Achilles fatally [3], though Ovid also presents an alternative account in which Apollo himself killed Achilles with arrows [4]. Apollodorus notes that Achilles had been forewarned by his mother Thetis that he would die by the hand of Apollo if he killed Tenes, which he did anyway [5]. However, Apollodorus also records a conflicting tradition that Achilles was killed by Alexander (another name for Paris) [6]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle.

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after his quarrel with Agamemnon because Agamemnon took his prize, Briseis. According to Homer's account, Achilles was angered that Agamemnon threatened to seize his prize despite Achilles bearing the brunt of the furious battle, while Agamemnon's own prize was far greater [1]. Feeling dishonored, Achilles declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain in such a state [1]. Apollodorus similarly reports that Achilles did not go forth to war because he was angry on account of Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [2].

Athena turned Arachne into a spider as punishment for her pride and defiance [1]. Arachne was a mortal weaver of extraordinary skill who refused to acknowledge Athena's superiority in the art of weaving [1]. When Athena appeared to her in disguise as an old woman and advised her to pray for the goddess's pardon, Arachne scornfully rejected the advice and challenged Athena herself to a weaving contest [1]. After they competed, Athena found Arachne's work flawless but was enraged by the girl's skill and defiance [2]. Athena destroyed Arachne's web and struck her repeatedly [2]. When Arachne attempted to hang herself in despair, Athena saved her life but pronounced a terrible doom: Arachne would dangle on a cord forever, and her punishment would last through all her descendants [3]. Athena then sprinkled her with magical herbs, transforming her body into that of a spider, condemning her to weave webs for eternity as a permanent reminder of the consequences of presuming to rival a goddess [3].

responses_by_run.answer: Prometheus was punished for two main transgressions: stealing fire and giving it to humans, and for attempting to deceive Zeus during a dispute over sacrificial portions at Mecone. According to Apollodorus, when Zeus learned that Prometheus had hidden fire in a stalk of fennel and given it to humans, Zeus ordered Hephaestus to bind him to Mount Caucasus, where an eagle devoured his liver daily [1]. Hesiod provides additional context, indicating that Prometheus had tried to trick Zeus by presenting him with bones covered in fat while giving the better portions to mortals, and that Zeus punished him for matching his wit against the almighty son of Cronos [2][3].

responses_by_run.answer: Odysseus escaped Polyphemus through a two-part plan [1][2]. First, he blinded the Cyclops by sharpening a wooden club, heating it in fire, and driving it into Polyphemus's eye with the help of his comrades [1][2]. When Polyphemus cried out for help from other Cyclopes, they misunderstood his cries of "Nobody" and departed [1][2]. Then, when Polyphemus opened the cave door to let his flocks out to pasture, Odysseus tied the sheep together in groups of three and hid beneath the largest ram, escaping with his comrades as the sheep passed out of the cave [1][2]. After reaching the ships, Odysseus sailed away and taunted the Cyclops by revealing his true name [1][2].

citations.author: Homer
citations.stance: describes Odysseus blinding Polyphemus and escaping via the sheep

citations.author: Homer
citations.stance: describes Odysseus taunting Polyphemus after escape

responses_by_run.answer: At the wedding of Peleus and Thetis, the gods celebrated the marriage with feast and song on Mount Pelion [1]. The marriage took place after Peleus seized Thetis despite her shape-shifting resistance, holding her fast as she transformed into fire, water, and various beasts until she resumed her original form [1]. Chiron gave Peleus an ashen spear as a wedding gift, and Poseidon gave him immortal horses named Balius and Xanthus [1].

Demeter stopped the crops from growing because she was grieving and yearning for her daughter Persephone, who had been taken away [1]. She vowed that she would never let fruit spring out of the ground until she beheld her daughter again [2].

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's grief and yearning for Persephone

citations.author: Anonymous ("Homeric")
citations.stance: describes Demeter's vow to prevent fruit from growing

responses_by_run.answer: The Olympians who are children of Cronus include Zeus [1][2][3][4][5], Poseidon [1][2][3], Hera [1], and Demeter [1][2]. These six deities are consistently identified across multiple ancient sources as the offspring of Cronus, the Titan who ruled before his children overthrew him to establish the Olympian order.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Cronus' AND child.type = 'olympian'

responses_by_run.answer: Several heroes are identified as children of Zeus in classical sources. Aeneas [1] is described as a son of Zeus, as is Arcesilaus [2]. Additionally, Iasion [3] is counted among Zeus's offspring.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT child.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, r.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM relationships r
JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = r.source_id
WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of' AND parent.name ILIKE 'Zeus' AND child.type = 'hero'
ORDER BY child.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Perseus encountered several monsters and adversaries throughout his adventures. The most famous was the Gorgon Medusa, whom he slew and beheaded [1]. Beyond this primary monster, Perseus used Medusa's severed head as a weapon against other foes. He turned Phineus and his fellow conspirators to stone by displaying the Gorgon's head [2][3]. Similarly, he turned Polydectes and his gathered friends to stone by showing them the Gorgon's head [2][3]. During the battle at Seriphus, Perseus also encountered and slew various human opponents, including Erithus (son of Actor), Caucasian Abaris, Polydaemon, Sperchius, Lycetus, Elyces, Clytus, and Phlegias [1]. Additionally, Molpeus and Ethemon pressed him during Phineus's attack [1].

Zeus's lineage traces back through several divine generations to Chaos itself. According to Hesiod's cosmological account, Earth (Gaia) emerged as a primordial deity [1], and from Earth came Cronus [2], who became Zeus's father. Cronus is consistently attested across multiple sources as a Titan who ruled before Zeus [3][4][5][6]. The genealogical chain thus connects Zeus through his father Cronus to the primordial Earth, which in turn represents a foundational level of divine existence in the Greek mythological cosmos that ultimately traces to Chaos as the first principle of creation.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Zeus'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM lineage
LEFT JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC, lineage.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: The following entities have the 'olympian' type: Aphrodite, Apollo, Ares, Artemis, Athena, Demeter, Dionysus, Hephaestus, Hera, Hermes, Poseidon, and Zeus.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
SELECT e.id, e.name, e.type, e.generation, e.domain, e.subtype
FROM entities e
WHERE e.type = 'olympian'
ORDER BY e.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: The provided material does not contain information about which heroes had a divine parent and died at Troy.
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH heroes_with_divine_parents AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT child.id, child.name
    FROM relationships r
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    JOIN entities child ON child.id = r.to_id
    WHERE r.relation = 'parent_of'
      AND parent.type IN ('primordial', 'titan', 'olympian', 'other_god')
      AND child.type = 'hero'
)
SELECT h.name AS name,
       s.author AS author, s.work AS work, vc.passage_ref AS passage_ref, s.stance AS stance
FROM heroes_with_divine_parents h
JOIN variant_claims vc ON vc.subject_entity_id = h.id
JOIN sources s ON s.id = vc.source_id
WHERE vc.claim_type = 'death'
  AND vc.claim_value ILIKE '%Troy%'
ORDER BY h.name ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles' divine lineage connects to Zeus through multiple ancestral lines. On his paternal side, Achilles descended from Zeus through his father Peleus, whose own father Aeacus was the son of Zeus and the mortal woman Aegina [1]. On his maternal side, Achilles was the son of Thetis, a Nereid (sea-nymph) and goddess in her own right [1]. According to the sources, when Peleus seized Thetis, she initially resisted by changing her shape, but eventually yielded and became his wife [2][3]. Their union produced Achilles, making him part-god through both his parents' lines—descended from Zeus patrilineally through Aeacus, and born of the divine Thetis maternally [1].
responses_by_run.sqlGenerated:
WITH RECURSIVE lineage AS (
    SELECT e.id, e.name, e.type, NULL::text AS source_id, NULL::text AS passage_ref,
           1 AS depth, ARRAY[e.id] AS visited
    FROM entities e
    WHERE e.name ILIKE 'Achilles'

    UNION ALL

    SELECT parent.id, parent.name, parent.type, r.source_id, r.passage_ref,
           lineage.depth + 1, lineage.visited || parent.id
    FROM lineage
    JOIN relationships r ON r.to_id = lineage.id AND r.relation = 'parent_of'
    JOIN entities parent ON parent.id = r.from_id
    WHERE NOT parent.id = ANY(lineage.visited) AND lineage.depth < 20
)
SELECT lineage.depth AS generation_level,
       lineage.name AS entity_name,
       lineage.type AS entity_type,
       s.author AS author,
       s.work AS work,
       s.stance AS stance,
       lineage.passage_ref AS passage_ref
FROM lineage
JOIN sources s ON s.id = lineage.source_id
WHERE lineage.source_id IS NOT NULL
ORDER BY lineage.depth ASC

responses_by_run.answer: Aphrodite's parentage is presented differently across ancient sources. According to Homer in the Iliad, Aphrodite is the child of Dione [1], while other Homeric works present a different account: the Odyssey identifies her as the daughter of Zeus [2]. Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca similarly records conflicting traditions, describing her as both a child of Zeus and a child of Dione [3]. Hesiod's Theogony offers a distinct origin story, stating that Aphrodite was born from the sea foam formed by the severed members of Heaven, cast into the sea by Cronus [4]. The Homeric Hymns also identify her as the daughter of Zeus [5], as does Ovid in the Metamorphoses [6].

responses_by_run.answer: Io's father is identified differently across ancient sources. According to Apollodorus in the Bibliotheca, Io was the daughter of Iasus [1], though Apollodorus also notes that Hesiod and Acusilaus claimed she was the daughter of Piren [2], while Castor and many tragedians identified her father as Inachus [3]. Ovid, in the Metamorphoses, consistently presents Io as the daughter of Inachus [4].

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles died by being struck with an arrow. According to Homer, he was fated to be slain by a god and a mortal [1], and Hector, dying, foretold that Achilles would be slain by Paris and Phoebus Apollo at the Scaean gate [2]. Ovid describes how Paris shot an arrow that was guided by Apollo to strike Achilles fatally [3], though Ovid also presents an alternative account in which Apollo himself killed Achilles with arrows [4]. Apollodorus mentions that Thetis forewarned Achilles he would die by the hand of Apollo if he killed Tenes [5], and his Bibliotheca also records that Achilles was killed by Alexander (Paris) [6]. After his death, Achilles was buried with Patroclus in the White Isle.

responses_by_run.answer: Achilles withdrew from the fighting after Agamemnon took his prize, Briseis, the daughter of Chryses the priest [1][2]. In his confrontation with Agamemnon, Achilles expressed his anger over being dishonored, stating that while he undertook the brunt of furious battle, Agamemnon's prize was far greater while his own reward was small [1][2]. Achilles declared he would return home to Phthia rather than remain dishonored and pile up riches for Agamemnon [1][2].
